text.title: The Coldest Village on Earth: Oymyakon (-71°C, -96°F)
text.u: a frozen ball. If you don't move, you
freeze. Imagine a village where
temperatures drop as low as -71.2°
C. Here, spending just a few extra
minutes outside can lead to frostbite,
the loss of a limb, or even end of life.
Is it frostbite? Oh, yes. After staying
outside for just over 15 minutes, my
nose turned white. Ice crystals began
forming inside the cells and the tissue
started to die. If I had stayed out 5
minutes longer, my nose would have
suffered permanent tissue damage. When
people breathed outdoors, it would sound
like dry grass rustling behind them.
That was the sound of warm breath
turning into tiny ice crystals.
I'm in the coldest inhabited place on
Earth at the coldest time of the year.
This is Oyukan. Just to stay alive, I'm
wearing more than 20 layers of clothing
weighing nearly 14 kilos. Stepping
outside from a warm 30° C home means
facing a temperature drop of over 100°.
That's why I'm also wearing gloves,
boots, pants, and a coat, all made from
reindeer fur. And even that's not
enough. And now I'm about to show you
what life is like when simply going
outside becomes a battle for survival.
Within seconds outside, ice crystals
start forming on the eyelashes and the
skin starts to sting. The village feels
like it's been abandoned.
The cold burned my finger. The cold
doesn't just affect people and wild
animals. It stops everything from
functioning. Bones don't work properly
and often shut down. Cars left outside
freeze in just a few hours. Since the
ground is always frozen, no fruits or
vegetables can grow here. We boarded a
plane in Moscow, flying east for 7 hours
to Yakutsk. From there, we covered an
18-hour, 900 km road trip. It's 2:00
a.m. and we finally arrived at the
coldest village in the world. The
thermometer reads -60.5°.
When we stepped out of the car, it felt
like we were on another planet.
The hairs in our nose were frozen, and
we felt them prick with every breath.
The dry air makes us cough
uncontrollably.
Omukan is home to around 800 people
spread across 1.5 km. Seeing anyone else
outside is almost impossible as if time
itself has frozen in this place. It's
really cold. We are walking for about 15
minutes. As you see, my friend's
eyelashes are full of ice. You can see
another ice droplet on his upper lip.
Our land surrounded by mountains. It
gets brutally cold here. The temperature
drops to -63 degrees C or even -64
degrees C. Because Omiaon is located in
a basin, there's hardly any wind. The
cold just settles in and stays. In this
region, the ground stays frozen all year
round. This layer of frozen earth is
called perafrost, and it can reach
hundreds of meters deep. To survive the
cold, the villagers built a central
heating system. A single coal powered
boiler sends heat to most homes through
pipes. The system must run nonstop. Even
a short failure puts everyone at risk.
The interiors of the houses differ from
traditional village homes. Heating pipes
connected to the central boiler run
through the rooms to keep the houses
warm. To build on the frozen ground,
thick wooden beams are first laid down
to create a flat base. Then the main
logs go on top. A 30 cm thick subfloor
is added, followed by insulation, and
finally the finishing floor. Each wall
is made of seven layered materials.
There's wood on the outside, then basalt
wool as the main insulation barrier,
then plaster, mesh, foam insulation,
another layer of plaster, and finally a
wooden panel inside. The windows are
triple glazed, forming a vital barrier
against the bone chilling temperatures,
and the attic is insulated with sawdust
and soil. Combined with a stove or
heating system, this setup keeps the
residents warm. In nearly every
building, the entrance room is
intentionally left unheated.
We freeze everything in the front room.
We get this refrigerator natural. Here I
have frozen milk and frozen meat that I
store in the cold. As you can see, these
first two rooms at the entrance used for
storage and as a refrigerator. These
unheated rooms also serve as a buffer
against the freezing cold. You can see
ice and snow covering the interior of
the room, especially the ceiling. In
Omukan, the day begins early. Yodokia is
already at the barn. Cattle farmers here
wrap a cloth around the cow's udder to
protect the sensitive tissue from
frostbite and to keep it clean.
The udder isn't at risk of freezing
anymore, but once the milk starts
flowing, we have to be careful. Because
of the extreme cold in Omukan, no water
pipes run to the buildings. They would
freeze almost instantly. The cows must
step out into the cold every morning and
make their way to the river to drink.
One by one, they step into an icy hell,
moving quickly, knowing they must return
before the cold becomes too harsh. Their
hair became crusted with ice almost
immediately. This part of the river is
not frozen because it is coming fresh
from the mountain underground spring
water meet and keeps this part of the
river unfrozen. Then all of a sudden, my
whole face went numb. I couldn't move
the muscle on the left side like I was
having facial paralysis.
M when my nose started freezing noticed
right away.
Is it frostbite? Yeah. The cold shrinks
the blood vessels and eventually reduces
blood circulation. Without enough
warmth, water inside each cell freezes
into ice crystals, causing the cells to
rupture and triggering a stabbing pain
and tissue to die.
You should cover your face like this.
Walk while covering your face.
Be careful or it might turn black. From
this point on until the end of the
documentary shoot, I won't be able to
stay outside for more than 10 minutes at
a time, or my already frostbitten nose
could get even worse. But as the
documentary progresses and the
temperatures drop even further, we'll be
pushing our limits to show you what
survival here truly demands.
Here is the cure. This is aloe and just
alcohol.
You soak it like this and apply it to
the area that's frozen. And don't rub
it, okay? Otherwise, you'll damage the
vessels in the capillaries and it'll
lead to sores. Then do began her daily
chores by hanging freshly washed clothes
in the yard. Within just minutes, they
completely froze solid. Her clothes are
completely frozen.
My hands are freezing.
After that, she headed into the barn to
milk the cows.
I keep yakoot cows because they produce
really high quality milk. The fat
content can reach 7 to 8%.
As you can see, there are some ice and
snow covering the ceiling, but here is
warm. The sides are also covered in
snow. You can see some droplets from the
ceiling and it's because of the
humidity. The air is thick with the
smell of hay, warm milk, and livestock.
Now we are going to meet the fork
master. We're about to see the wild
Yakudian horses in their natural
habitat.
Here are the Yakut horses. The
conditions here are so harsh that only
they have been able to adapt. What is
the temperature today? It's somewhere
around -50° C here. It's also hard to be
outside for a long time.
We have a system of fences in the open
area. They mostly graze freely. These
Yakudian horses don't need heated
shelters as they can endure -70° C
outdoors day and night. Unlike regular
horses, they are short. This also helps
them to stay outside during winters
because short body means it can radiate
heat to a smaller area. Their body looks
very puffy, very rich in fat and round.
This is because of their thick fur and
also their horseshoes are very tall
allowing them to stand on this freezing
ground very long time. They're not
captured. They just being fed here.
Their body means food. This is horse
meat. They eat it here. As you can see,
it got a lot of fat. This is one packet,
a packet of a horse meat. This was
ordered and it's going to be sold to a
customer. This large pile of hay has
been stored since summer. It's the main
food supply for the horses during
winter.
Miky offered us a taste of his hor's
meat and liver, which is only eaten raw
and frozen in this region. Raw horse
meat. It's full of vitamins and good
stuff.
Since we don't have fruit or vegetables
here, we get our vitamins from meat.
I like ice. I can't feel the taste of it
because it is frozen.
This is the liver in Oyakan. Daylight
lasts only about 4 and 1/2 hours in
January. It's 300 p.m. now. As darkness
falls, we're calling it a day.
Before cars, animals like horses,
cattle, and of course, reindeer were
used for transportation.
Reindeers are very important in this
region. Not just for transportation, but
also for its meat. skin and also milk.
About half a kilometer beyond the
village center, frozen forest of you can
begin. In this village, most people herd
animals for a living. But for those like
Ivonne, hunting becomes the main source
of income. I am a man of the forest.
These lands are my hunting grounds. And
here is my tent, my home.
He's making fire now. We need this fire
because it is too cold here.
We must walk on the path. The ice
underneath is thin. We made our way to
the frozen river to check the fishing
net he had set the day before. Ivonne
warned our cameraman just in time. He
was about to step onto a cracked patch
of ice. One wrong step could cause him
to fall into the water. Oh, hey. and get
wet. In the winter, when it was just as
cold, my dad was out riding his horse in
the woods. He fell through the ice and
started freezing right away. Realizing
that there was no warm shelter nearby
and no chance of rescue, he made the
decision to shoot himself to avoid a
slow, painful death.
Oh, someone was special. It's all
frozen. Our deer was already struggling
to work in this temperature. And now
this frozen lens is useless and we'll
have to give up on it. To get the net,
he's removing the ice from the hole
using a small shovel.
Just warm. The fish remained alive for
only a few seconds after touching the
freezing air. The water was warm, but
now on the surface, this fish is
shocked.
It's completely frozen. To make it
easier to cut, he's warming the frozen
fish by rubbing it with his hands.
He's carefully slicing it into thin
pieces. Frozen and raw wild fish. When
it's frozen, you feel like you're just
eating an ice.
You should eat with salt.
Fish are loaded with vitamins in the
winter. Inside the tent, it's warm, but
as you see, the outer part is completely
frozen.
He's feeding the fire
and sharing some of the fish to nature.
Oh spirit of nature, accept our gift.
Bless us with a successful hunt and
grace us with the abundance of your
land. According to his shamanic belief,
he must keep the spirit of the hunt
satisfied or next hunt he might come up
empty. We're heading into the
wilderness. Ivonne wants to hunt
something big.
Looks like something's been through
here. There are footprints and little
patches of urine left behind.
Look, roll deer tracks.
They're getting close. Because I stayed
out in the cold for more than 10
minutes, I've started to feel numbness
in my nose again. I have to be more
careful. I don't feel my
Okay.
From that side, it ran to a safaris.
That's where I'll set the trap.
He hopes to catch a large animal.
The hunter knows the animal follows the
same path every day, so he's placing the
trap along his daily route.
He's covering the trap with snow. He's
using a dog's tail to remove the traces
smoothly.
We'll return to this spot later to see
what he catches. But first, Ivonne wants
to check the trap he set yesterday. He
scans the snowy landscape carefully.
Everything here is like a book to me.
When the snow falls, I track how many
animals there are by their footprints. I
already know how many animals live in
this area. The Saka are a Turk people
who migrated from Central Asia. And I'm
from Turk, a country whose roots also
trace back to the same region. Before we
continue with the documentary, I'd like
to thank the sponsor of this project and
explore how closely I'm connected to
these people. I received this at home
DNA testing kit from this project
sponsor, My Heritage, to discover my
real ethnicity. It's super easy to use.
The kit comes with a swap. All I have to
do is rub it inside my cheek for about a
minute. Then I'm placing it in a while,
snapping it at the black line. I'm
packing everything up and sanding it to
return address. In about 4 weeks, my
results will be ready online, and I
can't wait to learn my roots. Finally,
the day has come. I got an email from My
Heritage. Let's see what my DNA says. To
be perfectly honest with you, I have no
idea what to expect. 47.3%
Greek and Albanian. It's almost hell. I
always knew that my grandma was from
Albania and now I have no doubt. Then
there is 40.5%
Turkish and 4.7% South Italy. My roots
stretch all the way to Central Asia. It
This is where Turkish people originally
come from. Then there are more slices of
other ethnicities. My heritage can even
pinpoint the exact locations my Turkish
ancestors came from. When I click here,
I can see my DNA matches. Here is
someone who shares 4% of my DNA. I don't
recognize this person, but he's listed
as my parents' first cousin. I also have
second and third cousins here. Actually,
more than 1,600 DNA relatives. Finding
out all this was so amazing. Huge thanks
to My Heritage for sponsoring this
video. If you want to discover your own
ethnicity, make sure to click the link
in the description box or scan the QR
code. Use the code RUHI29 to get your
DNA kit for just $29 or pound. That's a
huge 67% discount, the lowest price ever
offered. The DNA kits can be shipped
worldwide except Turkey, Brazil, and
France. However, everyone can still
build and track their own family tree
using My Heritage's Family Tree feature,
which comes with a 14-day free trial. My
Heritage matches your family tree with
34 billion records and millions of
family trees to find new discoveries for
you. Let's get back to the video now.
There it is lying on the snow. A solid
catch. You got a hair,
a very big one. Quite enough for all of
us to eat. It wasn't the trap that
killed it. The freezing cold did as he
lay there stuck and helpless. So, the
nature is no joke here. This trap is
smaller than the one we set today. I'm
curious to see what the next catch will
be. But for now, there will be meat on
the fire tonight.
In Omia, there are a lot of hairs.
During the war, it was actually because
of hunting hairs that our people
survived. Tamara cooked the hair and
prepared a dinner table at her house. It
doesn't have any fat in it. It's a very
lean meat.
Some of the upcoming scenes were among
the hardest to capture. Thanks to the
help of the Olgo, the leading YouTube
channel on rural Russia, we were able to
make it happen. At the first light of
morning, we come across something
unexpected near the house, a giant snake
sculpture. It's entirely made of cow
waste, weighing more than 360 kg. It was
built by a local farmer. The region's
sub-zero climate also makes plumbing
systems inoperable. If water pipes were
installed, the water inside would freeze
almost instantly. Instead, residents
meet their needs, such as washing their
hands from water they had previously
stored in tanks.
Our sink is old. There's a bucket
underneath to catch the water. When it
builds up, we just take it outside and
dump it. Once again, the absence of
plumbing infrastructure means that
houses have no indoor showers or
toilets. Instead, these essential
facilities are built in the gardens
inside unheated cabins. The lack of a
sewage system means that human waste
remains where it is accumulated. Since
waste freezes instantly upon exposure to
the air, it often accumulates into
frozen columns, sometimes reaching up to
1 or 2 meters in height before
eventually being broken down. When
someone has to use the bathroom, they
need to put on a lot of clothes and they
must be fast to not get frozen. Using
these toilets is never a pleasant
experience, but this one is different.
It's heated. But the problem with it, it
cannot be ventilated as the cold
disrupts the heating system.
This type of toilet is still preferred
to be protected from the cold despite
the unpleasant odor that accumulates
inside.
To bathe, cook, wash dishes, or simply
drink. Clean water is essential. But
getting it is a whole other process that
takes a lot of effort. People here
collect blocks of river ice and melt
them to get fresh water for daily use.
Right now, you're looking at a vast
white blanket of snow. But under there's
a frozen river, the only source of
water. He's using a chisel to cut into
the thick ice.
You have to work quickly if it's cold.
Otherwise, you'll freeze. As we carve
out solid blocks one after another, we
lift them and place them onto the sled.
We've loaded as many ice blocks as a
sled can carry.
Our
ancestors used to transport ice with
oxen.
How long these are going to last? I have
a big family. It'll last us two, maybe 3
weeks.
[Music]
These huge blocks are kept either in a
cold room inside the house or just
outside in the backyard. They put them
in a barrel and let them melt and then
they drink it.
The ice blocks we collected together
will not only be used in his own home,
but also shared with neighbors so they
can use the clean water to bathe.
In this village, a bathing routine
doesn't start with turning on a tap, but
with chopping wood.
The boy is grabbing a large chunk of ice
given by Nikolai and he's bringing it
inside.
Our grandparents take baths for 2 hours.
We usually stay for about an hour and a
half. How long does it take for you to
complete your bath? The whole process
takes around four or 5 hours. We bathe
once a week on Saturday or Sunday. The
next step is to turn frozen shampoo into
liquid near the stove.
They're rinsing themselves using a
ladle. When they finish, they're
dressing quickly as they'll step outside
from 60° C to -54° C, a difference of
114° C. After their weekly bath ritual
ends, the next morning begins early.
It's 7:30 a.m. and children are waking
up to a freezing cold morning, still
pitch black outside, thanks to the
simple indoor tank, the kids can wash
their faces and brush their teeth in the
warmth of the house.
But for other needs, the kids still have
to use the outdoor toilet. Jana helps
her daughters get dressed for school.
The children wear many layers of
clothing for their commute.
Before they leave, Jana wraps a wool
shaw tightly around their faces,
covering their mouths and noses to
protect them from the biting cold. Now
they're ready to go outside.
The walk to school is about half a
kilometer. Every winter morning, Nikolai
walks his daughters. The school remains
open in Orion unless temperatures drop
below bone chilling -55° C. Below that
point, it's too dangerous to go outside.
Today, the thermometer reads -51°.
As the kids arrive at school, daily life
for people like Ivonne is focused on
survival and hunting.
Ivon has got a lot of animals here. He
even has frozen wolf. He caught this
wolf before dawn about 2 hours ago in
the trap we set together.
It died because of the cold when it
couldn't move. If you don't move in the
cold, you die. This is a male one. Ian
will sell the skin of the wolf and take
the meat of it and make another trap.
Put the meat in the trap and he will
hunt a bear. He's showing us his hunting
collection. These are the pelts of the
foxes he tracked throughout the winter.
This one is a fully grown bear. These
bears were killed because they kept
attacking my livestock.
Every year they come onto my land and
each time there are more of them.
The cold in Orion is so extreme even the
most durable vehicles would fail without
proper preparation. If a vehicle is left
outside for even a few hours without the
engine running, it completely freezes
and won't start again without a full
defrosting process. How are you planning
to unfreeze your car? We'll use a jet
heater. We're not sure if it'll start.
Every part is frozen solid. Metal, fuel,
oil, even the battery. They start by
brushing off the heavy snow that's built
up on the car.
The tire has turned solid like a rock.
It was - 56° outside. The tire's gone
flat, not because of a leak, but because
cold temperatures cause the air
molecules inside to shrink.
They are going to install this to heat
the vehicle.
We try to close it tightly so that the
warm air does not escape out but stays
inside.
And take this one. It's too cold outside
because of this. Everything needs to be
kept warm.
Since the car is diesel, the diesel fuel
in the tank completely froze.
The car will stay like this for about 3
to four hours.
If a local is lucky enough to have a
heated garage, they can store their
vehicle there for months without risking
the engine. As the car was being
defrosted, we made our first stop at one
of these garages. The door is very well
insulated. There's no open spot in this
garage. They use this material.
If you see the walls, layer upon layer,
and there are pipes here to heat the
garage.
We heated the car for 3 hours. Now,
we're going to try starting. The
accumulator is installed.
And the oil level is checked using the
dipstick.
The first time opens the door.
Yeah.
Great.
This vehicle brought back from that.
Now we'll try to leave the place.
In Orion, survival isn't about
conquering nature, but about living in
harmony with it. Guided by ancient
shamanic wisdom, the Yakut people don't
fight the cold. They respect it and
endure within its rules. Look, the
temperature does not rise at all. Here,
even the crulest winters are met not
with fear, but with ritual and the
unshakable belief that nature gives as
much as it takes. This isn't just
survival, it's coexistence. Huge thanks
to my dear friend Hutky who was with me
every step of the way during this
journey. He also filmed a video in
Omukin where he accepted an
extraordinary invitation from a local
family and captured daily life in this
freezing village. Also, while filming
with Tur, we jumped into a frozen river
at -55° C. One of the most extreme
experiences of my life. If you want to
catch this unseen footage of the journey
and get a much closer look at the
region's culture and traditions, click
here to watch his documentary. Last but
not least, click the link in the
description or scan the QR code. And
don't forget to use my code RUHI29 to
get your My Heritage DNA kit for just
$29, or pounds. Don't miss this 67%
discount to discover your heritage.
